questions.subject: Polity
questions.prompt: Which of the following judicial pronouncements correctly matches the legal principle established regarding Personality Rights?

options.text: ICC Development Ltd. vs. Arvee Enterprises: Personality rights on the internet are distinct from reality.
Arun Jaitley vs Network Solutions: Taking away publicity rights violates Articles 19 and 21.
Rajinikanth v. Varsha Productions: Using a celebrity's style without consent violates personality rights.
Deepa Jayakumar v. AL Vijay: Personality rights are a form of permanent intellectual property.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct as the Madras HC in the Rajinikanth case ruled that using a celebrity's name, image, or style without consent violates personality rights. Option A is incorrect because ICC Development linked publicity rights to Articles 19 and 21. Option B is incorrect because the Arun Jaitley case established that fame on the internet is no different than in reality. Option D is incorrect as the case ruled that these rights end with the individual's lifetime.
questions.takeaway: Judicial precedents in India have expanded the scope of personality rights to include 'style' and 'likeness' while setting limits on their posthumous duration.
questions.evidenceExcerpt: Rajinikanth v. Varsha Productions (Madras HC, 2015): Using a celebrity’s name, image, or style without consent violates personality rights. ICC Development (International) Ltd. vs. Arvee Enterprises, 2003 (Delhi HC): Any effort to take away publicity right from individuals would be violative of Articles 19 and 21. Arun Jaitley vs Network Solutions Private Limited: Popularity or fame of an individual will be no different on internet than in reality.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.prompt: In the context of 'Posthumous Personality Rights' in India, which of the following statements is correct?

options.text: Personality rights are heritable and continue to be protected for 60 years after an individual's death under the Copyright Act.
The Madras High Court in the Deepa Jayakumar case held that privacy and reputation rights survive the individual and can be enforced by legal heirs.
The Emblems and Names (Prevention of Improper Use) Act, 1950, prohibits the commercial use of the images of certain national figures after their death.
India has a specific statutory framework that recognizes and protects the personality rights of all citizens posthumously.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the Emblems Act, 1950, provides a specific statutory prohibition against the commercial use of images of figures like Mahatma Gandhi and the Prime Minister. Option A and D are incorrect as there is no specific statutory recognition for posthumous personality rights for all citizens. Option B is incorrect because the Deepa Jayakumar v. AL Vijay (2019) judgment held that personality rights, reputation, and privacy come to an end after a person's lifetime.
questions.takeaway: Posthumous personality rights in India are generally not recognized, except for specific protections granted to national icons under the Emblems Act.
questions.evidenceExcerpt: Posthumous Personality Rights in India: No specific statutory recognition protecting Posthumous Personality Rights. However, Emblems Act, 1950 prohibits commercial use of images of Mahatma Gandhi and Prime Minister. Deepa Jayakumar v. AL Vijay (2019) Judgment: Personality right, reputation or privacy enjoyed by a person during his lifetime comes to an end after his or her lifetime.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.prompt: Which of the following correctly describes the special protection afforded to the Press regarding the interception of messages under Indian law?

options.text: All messages from registered news agencies are immune from interception to protect the freedom of speech.
Accredited press messages can only be intercepted if their transmission has been specifically prohibited.
Press messages are subject to the same interception rules as private citizens without any exceptions.
Only the Supreme Court can authorize the interception of messages belonging to accredited press correspondents.

questions.explanation: According to the legal safeguards, press messages accredited to the Central or State Government shall not be intercepted unless their transmission has been prohibited. This provides a conditional exemption for accredited press communications.
questions.takeaway: The law provides a qualified immunity for accredited press messages, making their interception conditional upon a specific prohibition of transmission.
questions.evidenceExcerpt: Exception for Press: Press messages accredited to Central or State Government shall not be intercepted unless their transmission has been prohibited.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.prompt: In the case of *ICC Development (International) Ltd. vs. Arvee Enterprises (2003)*, the Delhi High Court linked the 'Right to Publicity' to which of the following constitutional provisions?

options.text: Article 19 (Freedom of Speech) and Article 21 (Right to Life)

questions.explanation: The Delhi High Court in the ICC Development case held that any effort to take away the publicity right from individuals would be violative of Articles 19 and 21 of the Constitution, thereby grounding a commercial-style right in fundamental human rights.
questions.takeaway: Judicial interpretation in India has elevated the 'Right to Publicity' by anchoring it within the fundamental rights of freedom of expression and personal liberty.
questions.evidenceExcerpt: ICC Development (International) Ltd. vs. Arvee Enterprises, 2003 (Delhi HC): Any effort to take away publicity right from individuals would be violative of Articles 19 and 21.
